title: The Arrogance of Civilization is that it thinks everyone wants look like it
author: Arthur
content: The arrogance of a single model is thinking everyone wants to copy it, but the Pan-Africanist stands firm in knowing that our worth lies not in resembling others, but in reclaiming and building our own authentic self

title: The Future of Legal Tech in East Africa
content: Al, automation, and digital case management - how technology is reshaping the legal landscape in the region.

title: DNA Evidence in African Courts: A Colonial Relic That Must Be Rejected
author: Arthur Bwanika
content:
DNA Evidence in African Courts: A Colonial Relic That Must Be Rejected
Introduction
Across Africa, DNA testing is increasingly deployed as the supposed "gold standard" for resolving paternity, inheritance, and family disputes. Courts and legal practitioners have embraced this imported technology with an enthusiasm that masks a more troubling reality. Beneath the veneer of scientific objectivity lies a deeply problematic dynamic: DNA testing, as currently deployed in African legal systems, is a colonial relic that perpetuates the very power asymmetries colonialism established.

The introduction of DNA evidence into African courts cannot be understood outside the broader context of colonial legal imposition. During colonial rule, European legal frameworks were systematically imposed upon African societies, marginalizing and delegitimizing African customary law and traditional dispute resolution mechanisms. The colonial legal project was not merely about imposing new laws; it was about imposing a new epistemology—a new way of knowing and understanding the world. DNA testing represents the latest iteration of this dynamic—a technological "solution" imported from the Global North that displaces indigenous ways of knowing and determining kinship.

This article argues that African legal systems must critically re-examine and, in many instances, reject DNA evidence in favour of traditional African understandings of kinship and family. It is time to build legal frameworks that respect African sovereignty, African kinship, and African dignity—frameworks that use technology as a tool of justice, not a weapon of colonial domination.

The Colonial Roots of DNA Evidence in African Legal Systems
The extraction and use of African genetic data for DNA testing is part of a broader phenomenon that scholars have termed "bio-colonialism." Just as colonial powers extracted Africa's gold, oil, and labour, contemporary global powers are extracting African genomic data. The language of "universal medical progress" or "scientific advancement" masks an extractive logic that concentrates data-gathering in African populations while the profits flow to laboratories and corporations in the Global North.

Forensic science, including DNA analysis, was introduced to many African countries through colonial administrations. This colonial origin is not merely historical trivia; it has shaped the structures, assumptions, and power dynamics that continue to govern DNA evidence in African courts today. Current governance systems for genomic data in Africa "mirror colonial patterns in the control and sharing of genomic data". These patterns are reinforced not only by cross-border power asymmetries but also by internal power dynamics within countries, including institutional hierarchies and regulatory gaps that limit local decision-making authority.

The DNA testing industry, with its laboratories often based in Europe, South Africa, or North America, operates within this colonial framework. Samples are collected in Africa, transported abroad for analysis, and the results returned with minimal oversight. The legal and ethical frameworks that govern this process are inadequate, leaving African populations vulnerable to exploitation.

The historical context is instructive. Western science has long treated African bodies as objects of study rather than subjects of rights. From the ethically compromised medical trials that litter Africa's colonial history—including Pfizer's 1996 Kano trial where children were tested with an experimental antibiotic during a meningitis outbreak without proper consent, resulting in death, deafness, or brain damage—to contemporary genomic research initiatives that extract data with inadequate informed consent, the pattern is clear: African genetic material is treated as a resource to be extracted and exploited.

While the modus operandi of these operations once involved physical experimentation, today it is digital extraction. The question of informed consent deserves equal attention. When a rural farmer signs a complex legal document in a foreign language to provide a blood sample, we must question how truly voluntary that agreement is. Once data is digitised, its ownership—whether it belongs to the individual, the community, or the state—remains unclear under international law. This legal vacuum is being exploited.

The Illusion of Scientific Objectivity
Proponents of DNA testing in African courts present it as objective, neutral, and definitive. This is a dangerous illusion that masks the technology's inherent limitations and biases. DNA testing, particularly kinship testing conducted without direct samples from the alleged parent, is statistically inferential and subject to manipulation.

The scientific community itself acknowledges these limitations. Critics have raised persistent concerns about how far genomic data can, or should, be used to reconstruct social identities. DNA ancestry testing, in particular, has been criticized for reifying racial categories and omitting historical contexts such as colonialism and slavery. The tests are based on reference populations that do not adequately reflect African genetic diversity, yet courts routinely treat them as infallible.

The problems with DNA testing in African contexts are compounded by the underrepresentation of African populations in genetic databases. As the African Genome Variation Project, published in Nature, underscored, the continent holds the world's most genetically diverse population. Yet genomic research and testing infrastructure remain underdeveloped and inequitably distributed across the continent. This means DNA test results for Africans are inherently less reliable than for populations from the Global North—a scientific fact that courts routinely ignore.

When courts treat DNA testing as infallible, they are not applying science; they are applying a scientism that masks the technology's limitations and biases. Scientism is the belief that science alone can provide answers to all questions, coupled with a refusal to acknowledge science's social, political, and historical contexts. In African courts, scientism manifests as an uncritical acceptance of DNA evidence that would not be tolerated in the legal systems of the Global North.

The anonymity myth has also been exposed. A landmark study in Science showed that "anonymous" DNA can be traced back to individuals using nothing more than public genealogy databases, an age and a region. With advances in AI, algorithms can now reconstruct identities from fragments once considered negligible. The uncomfortable reality is that DNA is a permanent biological signature. Unlike a compromised password, a genetic code cannot be changed once exposed. What is shared today as aggregate statistical data could, tomorrow, become an instrument of surveillance—implicating not just an individual, but their entire family lineage.

The Erosion of African Customary Law
African customary law has long recognized that paternity and kinship are determined not solely by biology but by social recognition, care, and responsibility. A child born into a family, acknowledged by the father, and raised within the community is a member of that family. This understanding is rooted in the lived realities of African communities, where kinship is a social relationship, not merely a genetic fact.

Under African customary law, the acknowledgment of a child by a father during his lifetime is a powerful and legally significant act. When a man provides for a child, enrolls the child in his medical insurance, sends money for the child's upkeep, and publicly treats the child as his own, he has established a relationship that the law should recognize. A posthumous DNA test, conducted without the father's biological samples, should not be permitted to undo a lifetime of recognition.

DNA testing threatens to upend this traditional understanding. It reduces kinship to biology, ignoring the social, cultural, and historical dimensions that have always defined African family relationships. It imposes a Western, individualistic conception of family on African communities that have long understood family as a web of social relationships extending beyond biological connections.

In traditional common law and customary law, there is the presumption that a child born in wedlock is presumed legitimate, thus belonging to the husband of the marriage even though biologically, he may not. This presumption reflects a social understanding of family that DNA testing threatens to displace. The imposition of DNA testing in inheritance disputes represents a fundamental assault on African customary law and the autonomy of African communities to determine their own kinship systems.

Scholars have noted that genomics raises questions around identity, sovereignty, marriageability, and the ethics of disclosure that cannot be resolved through a "one size fits all" model. The tension between Western science ideologies and indigenous peoples' cultural beliefs in relation to their human genetic resources and data is well-documented. Indigenous groups throughout the world have filed numerous declarations denouncing research that disregards their sovereignty over their human genetic resources.

Bio-Colonialism: The New Frontier of Exploitation
The extraction of African genetic data for DNA testing is part of a broader phenomenon that scholars Nick Couldry and Ulises Mejias argue is the modern-day equivalent of historical land grabs. They note that under this system, "what is now being appropriated is human life through its conversion into data".

The downstream applications of genetic data extend well beyond the clinic. In the hands of insurance corporations or financial institutions, a population's genetic map ceases to be a tool of care and becomes a ledger of risk. If DNA can predict a predisposition to illness, that information can exclude individuals from economic participation before they have fallen ill: insurance premiums tied to genetic markers, jobs decided by biological risk scores, entire communities written off as unprofitable. In the criminal justice system, people could be labelled as dangerous or high-risk simply because of their genes before they have done anything wrong.

African nations are increasingly pushing back against this exploitation. In February 2026, Zimbabwe rejected a $367 million U.S. health deal, correctly identifying the agreement as a demand for "raw materials for scientific discovery" without ensuring that Zimbabweans would ever access the resulting treatments. In late April 2026, Ghana officially walked away from a $109 million U.S. health deal, refusing to surrender citizens' personal data. As one source close to the Ghanaian government noted bluntly: "The deal is dead".

The concept of "human genomic sovereignty" offers a promising framework for African communities seeking to protect their genetic resources. This concept recognizes that indigenous peoples have the right to regulate research and the generation of knowledge about their populations and resources by invoking sovereignty over their human genetic resources. Lessons can be drawn from indigenous groups in the USA and Canada in the quest for human genomic sovereignty in Africa.

The Harm to Vulnerable Populations
The deployment of DNA testing in African courts disproportionately harms the most vulnerable: women and children. In paternity and inheritance disputes, women are often forced to subject themselves and their children to invasive testing to prove what they already know. Children born outside of formal marriage—already stigmatized—face the additional burden of having their legitimacy questioned through a "scientific" process they did not consent to.

The power dynamics are deeply unequal. The party seeking to exclude a child from inheritance can demand a DNA test, and the child and mother must comply or risk losing their claim. The test itself, if it excludes the child, can be used as a weapon to silence the mother and erase the child's identity. The psychological harm to children subjected to DNA testing cannot be overstated; they are being told, in the most explicit and "scientific" terms, that they do not belong to their family.

The harm extends to entire communities. When DNA testing is used to determine inheritance, it disrupts the social fabric of families and communities. Old wounds are reopened; disputes that customary mechanisms could have resolved are escalated; families are torn apart. The cost is not just financial but psychological, social, and cultural.

Furthermore, the threat of criminal prosecution looms over those who challenge DNA results. Individuals are threatened with criminal and civil prosecution if they continue to claim a child is the deceased's daughter without a contrary scientific report. This is not justice; it is intimidation using the tools of colonial science.

Legal Frameworks That Perpetuate Injustice
The legal frameworks governing DNA evidence in African courts are themselves part of the problem. Many African countries have adopted evidence laws based on colonial models that privilege scientific evidence over other forms of proof. The Evidence Act, in many jurisdictions, creates a hierarchy of evidence in which DNA is treated as superior to eyewitness testimony, documentary evidence, or traditional forms of proof.

This hierarchy is not neutral. It reflects a colonial epistemology in which Western science is deemed superior to African knowledge systems. The uncritical acceptance of DNA evidence perpetuates this hierarchy and delegitimizes African ways of knowing.

Furthermore, the procedural rules governing DNA evidence often fail to protect the rights of the vulnerable. There may be no requirement for independent oversight of DNA testing; no requirement that the testing be conducted by an accredited laboratory; no requirement that the results be subject to independent verification. The burden of challenging DNA evidence often falls on the party seeking to exclude it, placing an unreasonable burden on the most vulnerable.

African nations currently share minimal, if any, forensic DNA profiles, in contrast to the European Union. Only until a significant number of African countries perform DNA analysis and create national forensic DNA databases will forensic DNA sharing become a reality and assist in cross-border initiatives. This disparity reflects the broader inequities in the global forensic science landscape.

What Must Be Done
It is not enough to simply reject DNA testing in African courts. A constructive approach requires comprehensive reform at multiple levels.

First, African courts must critically scrutinize DNA evidence, particularly kinship tests conducted without direct samples from the alleged parent. Such tests are inferential, not definitive, and courts must treat them as such. The burden of proof must remain on those seeking to use DNA evidence to establish or challenge paternity. Courts must require proof of chain of custody, accreditation of laboratories, and verification of results.

Second, African countries must develop sovereign frameworks for genomic data governance that protect their populations from exploitation. Current governance systems that mirror colonial patterns must be replaced with local or co-ownership models that reflect African interests. This requires rethinking governance structures and promoting approaches that support decolonized approaches to data governance.

Third, customary law must be respected and integrated into the legal framework. Where a deceased person acknowledged a child during their lifetime through support, care, and recognition, that acknowledgment should carry significant weight—potentially more weight than a posthumous DNA test conducted without the deceased's biological samples. Courts must be guided by the principle that kinship is a social relationship, not merely a biological fact.

Fourth, informed consent for DNA testing must be genuine and meaningful. Consent must be obtained in languages that participants understand, with full disclosure of the nature and limitations of the test, the identity of the testing laboratory, and the uses to which the results may be put. The exploitation of vulnerable populations through inadequate consent must be criminalized.

Fifth, African legal systems must invest in their own forensic and genomic capacities, reducing dependence on foreign laboratories and ensuring that DNA testing, where it is used, is conducted to the highest scientific and ethical standards. This includes accreditation of local laboratories, training of local forensic scientists, and development of local reference databases that reflect African genetic diversity.

Sixth, African governments must engage in international advocacy to challenge the extractive practices of foreign DNA testing companies. This includes demanding transparency in pricing, accountability in testing procedures, and respect for African legal and ethical standards. The principle of "nothing about us without us" must guide all genomic research and testing involving African populations.

Seventh, African legal education must be reformed to include critical perspectives on DNA evidence. Lawyers, judges, and magistrates must be trained to understand the limitations and biases of DNA testing and to evaluate it in the context of African legal traditions and customary law.

Eighth, the concept of human genomic sovereignty must be embraced and operationalized. Indigenous groups in Africa can draw lessons from the USA and Canada in their quest for human genomic sovereignty. An Indigenous Jurisprudential Framework that integrates customary law and stewardship principles could create a culturally relevant governance model for genomic and biobanking research.

A Vision for Decolonized Justice
The call to reconsider DNA evidence in African courts is part of a broader project of decolonizing African legal systems. This project requires a fundamental rethinking of the epistemological foundations of African law—a shift from imported Western paradigms to African-centred frameworks.

Decolonized justice recognizes that law is not neutral; it reflects the values, interests, and power structures of those who create it. The uncritical acceptance of DNA evidence reflects the continuing power of Western science and the continuing marginalization of African knowledge systems. Decolonized justice challenges this power imbalance and insists on the recognition of African ways of knowing.

Decolonized justice recognizes that kinship is a social relationship, not merely a biological fact. It values social recognition, care, and responsibility as the foundations of family relationships. It respects the autonomy of African communities to determine their own kinship systems and rejects the imposition of foreign standards.

Decolonized justice protects the most vulnerable: women, children, and marginalized communities. It refuses to subject them to invasive testing and intimidation. It ensures that the burden of proof does not fall unfairly on those with the least resources.

Decolonized justice is justice for all, not just for those with the power to demand DNA tests. It is justice that respects African traditions while embracing the best of modernity. It is justice that is rooted in African soil, reflects African values, and serves African communities.

Conclusion
DNA testing, as currently deployed in African courts, is a colonial relic that perpetuates the extractive logic of colonialism. It displaces African customary law, exploits vulnerable populations, and subjects African genetic material to extraction by foreign interests. The pattern is clear: from the ethically compromised medical trials that litter Africa's history to contemporary genomic data extraction, African genetic material has been treated as a resource to be exploited.

The call to reconsider DNA evidence in African courts is not a call to reject science. It is a call to decolonize science—to ensure that technology serves African communities rather than exploiting them, to respect African customary law rather than displacing it, and to protect the most vulnerable rather than subjecting them to further harm.

It is time to build legal frameworks that respect African sovereignty, African kinship, and African dignity. It is time to reject the colonial relic of uncritical DNA evidence and embrace a vision of justice that is truly African—a vision that recognizes the humanity and dignity of every person, a vision that protects the vulnerable and respects the traditions of the community, a vision that uses technology as a tool of justice, not a weapon of colonial domination.

Africa's genome is not for sale. Neither is Africa's justice system. The time has come to reclaim both.

The author is a legal scholar specializing in African customary law and bioethics. The views expressed are his own.

Sources and Further Reading
Ekusai-Sebatta, D. et al. (2026). "Decolonizing genomic data ownership: a qualitative study of legal practices and access in Uganda." BMC Medical Ethics. 

"Africa's genome is not for sale." TRT Afrika (2026). 

"Africa's genome is not for sale." Newswatch (Nigeria) (2026). 

"The Sovereignty Paradox: Data Colonialism, Debt, and the Battle for Africa's Digital Nervous System." Imani Africa (2026). 

Kabata, F. (2024). "Indigenous Peoples' human genomic sovereignty: Lessons for Africa." Developing World Bioethics. 

"Decolonising forensic odontology in Sub-Saharan Africa." PMC (2025). 

"From Country Marks to DNA Markers: The Genomic Turn in the Reconstruction of African Identities." Cambridge Repository.

title: The Colonial Education Legacy and Uganda's Unemployment Crisis Introduction
content:
The Colonial Education Legacy and Uganda's Unemployment Crisis
Introduction: A System Not Designed for Its People
Every graduation season in Uganda paints a familiar picture: thousands of proud students celebrating academic achievement, clutching certificates meant to unlock their future. Yet months later, many of those same graduates are job-hunting or selling items on the streets to survive. Uganda produces over 700,000 graduates from universities and polytechnics each year—far more than the number of formal jobs created.

This crisis is not an accident. It is the predictable outcome of an education system imported by colonialism and preserved by post-independence governments—a system designed to produce clerks, interpreters, and administrators for empire, not innovators, entrepreneurs, or problem-solvers for an independent nation.

I. The Colonial Foundation of Uganda's Education System
From Indigenous Learning to Missionary Imposition
Long before Europeans arrived, education in Uganda was communal, practical, and deeply rooted in community life. Young people learned through apprenticeship and practice—farming, hunting, fishing, iron smelting, crafts, and social organization. Every individual contributed to community survival, and unemployment was virtually unknown.

This changed in 1877 with the arrival of Christian missionaries, who introduced what they called "formal education." The system they established carried a clear agenda: spread Christianity and produce African collaborators who could serve colonial administration. As a 1978 study by scholar D. Mudoola documented, colonial officials were deliberately cautious about higher education for Africans. There was a general fear that if Africans acquired a higher "literary education," they would "imbibe values which they could use as intellectual tools to critically re-examine the colonial order".

The Architecture of Control: How the System Was Built
The colonial education structure was deliberately designed to create dependence and limit ambition:

1. Religious Division and Exclusion: President Yoweri Museveni, reflecting on his education at Ntare School, condemned the sectarian system that divided schools along religious lines. Protestant-founded schools like Gayaza High School and King's College Budo, Catholic institutions like St. Mary's College Kisubi and Namilyango College, competed with and were hostile to each other. Muslim children were the most affected—denied admission to both systems. As Museveni noted, "The Muslims, as a result, could not get clerical jobs, join the civil service, or even work as office messengers. They ended up in petty businesses like butchery and truck driving".

2. Language as a Weapon of Control: The British imposed English as the medium of instruction, deliberately privileging those who could master the colonizer's tongue while marginalizing indigenous languages and knowledge systems. Command of English became "a critical qualification for attaining high official position", creating a narrow elite dependent on colonial systems.

3. Literate v. Practical Education: Missionary schools emphasized cramming, memorization, and content recall—producing students who could pass exams but lacked practical competence. This was not accidental. A system that emphasizes obedience and memorization over independent thought produces workers, not thinkers or innovators.

4. Deliberate Limitation of Aspiration: Colonial officials actively resisted expanding higher education because they saw it as "a destabilizing factor to colonial hegemony". Education was calibrated to produce enough administrators to run the colony—not enough critical thinkers to challenge it.

II. The Legacy: How Colonial Education Creates Unemployment
A Mismatch Between Training and Market Reality
More than 60 years after independence, Uganda still operates largely within this inherited framework. The consequences are visible across the economy:

1. Cramming over Competence: Dr. Monica Musenero, Minister of Science, Technology, and Innovation, told the Education Policy Review Commission that the current system "produces students who are assessed on the basis of who can cram content than on the basis of producing value needed to solve problems for communities". Students memorize to pass exams, then graduate unable to apply knowledge in practical settings.

2. White-Collar Prestige v. Practical Skills: The colonial system created immense social prestige around formal employment—clerical work, teaching, administration. Meanwhile, agriculture, craftsmanship, and small-scale enterprise were systematically devalued. This mindset persists today: graduates wait years for office jobs that may never come rather than creating enterprises or applying skills in productive sectors.

3. Content v. Skill Focus: A study on neo-colonialism and education found that the current O-Level History curriculum "is too westernized and portrays a colonial model...most History Education content is not skill based and thus not realistic in our society," leading to "high skyrocketing unemployment".

4. Dependency on External Models: Uganda continues to rely heavily on foreign funding, experts, and priorities in shaping education policy. Professor Anthony Mugagga of Makerere University noted that even the recent Education Policy Review Commission showed "clear signs of Western influence, particularly through the involvement of foreign countries that provided both experts and funding".

The Numbers Tell the Story
According to the Uganda Bureau of Statistics 2024 census, Uganda's population stands at 45.9 million, with half below 18 and 23.5% between 18 and 30 years old. Yet 50.9% of youth are not in employment, education, or training. Despite decades of expanding access—more than 51,000 primary and secondary schools and a 72% literacy rate—the system fails to prepare graduates for economic participation.

III. Reform Efforts and Their Limitations
The Cycle of Reports, Not Revolution
Uganda has cycled through multiple education reform commissions without addressing the fundamental colonial structure:

The Senteza Kajubi Report (1989-1992) led to Universal Primary Education (UPE) in 1997 and greater emphasis on vocational education. Yet the colonial framework remained intact.

The Mushega Report (2025), hailed by Education Minister Janet Museveni as "game-changing," proposed reforms including local language instruction up to P4, expanded vocational training, and abolishing PLE in favor of continuous assessment. However, critics argue it "adjusts the system without transforming its foundation," leaving the colonial structure untouched.

Political sociologist Gertrude Kamya Othieno describes this as "patching up a colonial model that was never ours to begin with." She argues: "Reform will not save us. Only a revolution will".

The Gap Between Policy and Practice
Even promising reforms face implementation barriers. The shift toward a competency-based curriculum, while overdue, has struggled with what experts call an "implementation gap"—the gap between intended and actual implementation. As Professor Namubiru noted, many "people with briefcases" (foreign experts and funders) still heavily influence the "cooking pot" of Uganda's curriculum development where core content is approved.

IV. Can Uganda Decolonize Its Education?
Voices Calling for Transformation
A growing movement of experts and policymakers argues for fundamental change:

1. Integrating Indigenous Knowledge: Professor Proscovia Namubiru, Acting Deputy Vice Chancellor at UNITE, advocates integrating indigenous knowledge into the curriculum. She notes that pre-colonial African knowledge in agriculture, social organization, and craftsmanship was systematically suppressed by missionaries who branded it as "satanic" or "primitive." She points to examples like "Okwepena," a traditional game that teaches communication, numeracy, problem-solving, conflict resolution, and teamwork—skills essential in both personal and professional life.

2. Valuing Local Languages: Experts argue that African languages must become central to instruction. China teaches in Mandarin, Japan blends technology with traditional craftsmanship, and South Korea abandoned its colonial education system to build a knowledge economy rooted in its language and culture.

3. Shifting from Academics to Economic Self-Reliance: Dr. Musenero proposed a system "based on industrial value chains," allowing children to "discover, create, innovate, socialize and take care of society and nature right from the lower levels of learning." She asked: "What if our educational system encouraged students to use their land to develop a novel idea? I believe that even if one did not attend a four-wall classroom, they would still be superior to today's degree holders".

Progress and Persistent Barriers
There is some movement. Uganda's new lower secondary curriculum reduced Western-centric content: 64% of content is now local, 30% African, and just 6% Western. History and geography topics on Europe and North America were significantly reduced.

However, barriers remain formidable:

Foreign funding continues to shape education priorities.

Political will for radical change faces resistance from those benefiting from the current system.

The social prestige attached to white-collar jobs remains deeply embedded.

Implementation capacity lags behind policy ambition.

V. Conclusion: A System Designed for Empire Cannot Build a Nation
The link between Uganda's education system and its unemployment crisis is not coincidental—it is causal. A system designed to produce clerks for colonial administration cannot produce entrepreneurs for an independent economy. An education that prizes cramming and obedience over critical thinking cannot foster innovation. A curriculum that ignores local knowledge and prioritizes foreign content cannot prepare graduates to solve local problems.

The unemployed graduates of Kampala are not failures of the system; they are its intended output. The tragedy is not that the system fails them—it is that it was never designed to succeed for them in the first place.

As President Museveni bluntly stated, "Uganda's education, which was built on a colonial legacy, is irrelevant to the country's needs". The question is whether Uganda will continue reforming the prison walls or finally tear them down and build something new.

The Mushega Report and its predecessors represent the reformist path. But as Othieno argues, "Reform will not save us. Only a revolution will"—one that places African languages at the core of learning, teaches history through an African lens, and builds an economy based on self-reliance, not dependency.

Until that revolution comes, Uganda will continue to produce graduates who cannot find work in an economy their education never prepared them to build

Introduction: A System Not Designed for Its People
Every graduation season in Uganda paints a familiar picture: thousands of proud students celebrating academic achievement, clutching certificates meant to unlock their future. The shift toward a competency-based curriculum, while overdue, has struggled with what experts call an "implementation gap"—the gap between intended and actual implementation. As Professor Namubiru noted, many "people with briefcases" (foreign experts and funders) still heavily influence the "cooking pot" of Uganda's curriculum development where core content is approved.

IV. Can Uganda Decolonize Its Education?
Voices Calling for Transformation
A growing movement of experts and policymakers argues for fundamental change:

1. Integrating Indigenous Knowledge: Professor Proscovia Namubiru, Acting Deputy Vice Chancellor at UNITE, advocates integrating indigenous knowledge into the curriculum. She notes that pre-colonial African knowledge in agriculture, social organization, and craftsmanship was systematically suppressed by missionaries who branded it as "satanic" or "primitive." She points to examples like "Okwepena," a traditional game that teaches communication, numeracy, problem-solving, conflict resolution, and teamwork—skills essential in both personal and professional life.

2. Valuing Local Languages: Experts argue that African languages must become central to instruction. China teaches in Mandarin, Japan blends technology with traditional craftsmanship, and South Korea abandoned its colonial education system to build a knowledge economy rooted in its language and culture.

3. Shifting from Academics to Economic Self-Reliance: Dr. Musenero proposed a system "based on industrial value chains," allowing children to "discover, create, innovate, socialize and take care of society and nature right from the lower levels of learning." She asked: "What if our educational system encouraged students to use their land to develop a novel idea? I believe that even if one did not attend a four-wall classroom, they would still be superior to today's degree holders".

Progress and Persistent Barriers
There is some movement. Uganda's new lower secondary curriculum reduced Western-centric content: 64% of content is now local, 30% African, and just 6% Western. History and geography topics on Europe and North America were significantly reduced.

However, barriers remain formidable:

Foreign funding continues to shape education priorities.

Political will for radical change faces resistance from those benefiting from the current system.

The social prestige attached to white-collar jobs remains deeply embedded.

Implementation capacity lags behind policy ambition.

V. Conclusion: A System Designed for Empire Cannot Build a Nation
The link between Uganda's education system and its unemployment crisis is not coincidental—it is causal. A system designed to produce clerks for colonial administration cannot produce entrepreneurs for an independent economy. An education that prizes cramming and obedience over critical thinking cannot foster innovation. A curriculum that ignores local knowledge and prioritizes foreign content cannot prepare graduates to solve local problems.

The unemployed graduates of Kampala are not failures of the system; they are its intended output. The tragedy is not that the system fails them—it is that it was never designed to succeed for them in the first place.

As President Museveni bluntly stated, "Uganda's education, which was built on a colonial legacy, is irrelevant to the country's needs". The question is whether Uganda will continue reforming the prison walls or finally tear them down and build something new.

The Mushega Report and its predecessors represent the reformist path. But as Othieno argues, "Reform will not save us. Only a revolution will"—one that places African languages at the core of learning, teaches history through an African lens, and builds an economy based on self-reliance, not dependency.

Until that revolution comes, Uganda will continue to produce graduates who cannot find work in an economy their education never prepared them to build
